petitioner_background: The petitioner is a woman from Nepal who claims to work in the field of social work, with involvement in women's rights, political activism, and academia. She served as a lecturer at Tribhuvan University, held positions with the National Women's Commission (NWC) of Nepal, the Nepali Congress Party, Amnesty International Nepal, the Susma Koirala Memorial Trust, and the Cricket Association of Nepal. She sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of: (1) the Best Lady Jaycee of the Year award in 1982; (2) an award by the Nepalese Ministry of Home Affairs in 1996 (also referred to as the Silver Jublee Medal); (3) a certificate of participation with the Susma Koirala Memorial Trust in 1997; (4) a certificate of participation in a seminar held by the Nepali Congress Central Communication Department in 1998; and (5) a certificate of participation with a rally held by the Nepali Congress District Working Committee in 2002. An accompanying letter stated the Best Lady Jaycee award was national in scope.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the certificates of participation are not awards or prizes, but instead amount to recognition of the petitioner's involvement in the event, with no evidence that participation in any of these events conveys national or international recognition. Regarding the Best Lady Jaycee award, the AAO explained this criterion requires that the award be nationally or internationally recognized for excellence, not that it is awarded by an organization that is national or international or chooses the recipient from among a national or international pool. The petitioner submitted no secondary evidence such as news articles or letters from sponsoring organizations documenting the prestige of the award. Similarly, no evidence appeared in the record about the Silver Jublee Medal to indicate national or international recognition. Additionally, these two awards were given in 1982 and 1996, over a decade prior to the filing of this petition, and therefore cannot evidence the requisite sustained acclaim required for this highly restrictive classification.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of membership in: (1) the National Women's Commission (NWC), with rules stating the King appoints members based on recommendations by the prime minister, chief justice, opposition party leader, a high ranking woman in government, and the most senior member of Parliament, from a pool of women over 35 who have served in women's rights for at least 10 years with no criminal convictions; (2) the Susma Koirala Memorial Trust; (3) the Nepal University Teachers' Association (NUTA); (4) Amnesty International; (5) the Research Board of Advisors of The American Biographic Institute; and (6) the Central Policy and Program Committee of Nepal Women Association. She also claimed leadership positions with Amnesty International of Nepal and the Teachers' Association. Letters from Professor Bhupti Dhakal (president of NUTA) and Krishna Pahadi (chairperson of Amnesty International Nepal) were submitted, and a letter from Youdha Shah, Acting Chief Secretary, dated April 2002, indicated the petitioner was appointed to the NWC on March 7, 2002.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the NWC membership criteria do not require an outstanding achievement, nor do they indicate that the political officials who decide membership are national or international experts in social work or women's rights. Moreover, NWC members serve two-year tenures, so the petitioner (appointed in 2002) ceased being a member in 2004, nearly three years before filing. For the other organizations, the AAO found the information does not indicate that membership is predicated upon outstanding achievement or judged by recognized national or international experts. The NUTA letter referenced 'remarkable contribution' but did not state such contributions were required for membership. Amnesty International of Nepal's website indicated any Nepali citizen over 16 committed to its mission can be a member, with executive committee chosen by general vote rather than by experts. The petitioner served as a NUTA member and Amnesty International National Executive Committee member only from 1997-1999, well before filing. The AAO cited Matter of Katigbak for the principle that eligibility must be established at the time of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted articles appearing in Nari Magazine, Manchitra Magazine, and Kumari Magazine. She also submitted a letter from the Library of Congress identifying the publisher of Nari Magazine, and a letter from Gagadish Poudel of the Press Council Nepal stating that the three magazines 'fall almost under A grade and wide circulated.' She also submitted evidence of inclusion in the Asia/Pacific Who's Who.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the translations failed to demonstrate the entire articles were translated as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) — only excerpts or abstracts were submitted. The translations also did not include the author's name as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The petitioner presented no evidence that any of these magazines constitute a professional or major trade publication or other major media — the Library of Congress letter only identified the publisher, and did not contain information such as circulation or distribution numbers. The Press Council Nepal statement was too nebulous to establish the publications as major media. Only the article in Manchitra Magazine was properly considered under this criterion, as the other articles were authored by the petitioner and would be considered under criterion (vi). Regarding the Asia/Pacific Who's Who, appearing as one of thousands of successful individuals in a frequently published directory is not evidence of published material about the petitioner in major media nor indicative of national acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of: (1) service on the monitoring and evaluation team for the Social Welfare Council, including a letter from the Social Welfare Council member secretary and the Notice establishing the Social Welfare Council; (2) service on the interview board of Mahendra Multiple Campus (MMC), Tribhuvan University, with a letter from the Campus Chief of MMC stating interview board members decided who should be hired pending 'approval from Tribhuvan University'; and (3) service on Tribhuvan University's monitoring and evaluation team, with a letter from the Deputy Controller stating the petitioner conducted and monitored exams in the mid-western region in 2001.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found neither the Social Welfare Council member secretary letter nor the Notice establishing the Council included information about the petitioner's duties as part of the monitoring and evaluation team, and neither stated any member was engaged in judging any competition. No evidence indicated the petitioner took part in evaluating project proposals, nor were there criteria for how judging was to take place. For the MMC interview board, decisions were subject to University approval, and the evidence showed members were appointed based on performance and minimum 15 years of service — no evidence showed how the petitioner was chosen for the monitoring and evaluation team. The AAO cited 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(2) that judging must be consistent with sustained national or international acclaim at the very top of the field, noting that judging a national competition for top scientists is of greater probative value than judging a regional youth or student competition. Judging teacher appointments or monitoring exams was not sufficient to demonstrate judging the work of others in her claimed field of social work. Moreover, the service occurred in the late 90s or early 2000s, at least 7 years before filing, failing to demonstrate sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of: (1) involvement in a variety of conferences held by nonprofit and philanthropic organizations; (2) participation in the international convention of CEDAW in 2004 with subsequent advocacy of its issues upon return to Nepal; (3) five letters of recommendation, including from an NWC official describing her visiting fifteen districts and handling women's abuse cases, from a Tribhuvan University official describing her conducting exams in the mid-western region in 2001, from a professor at Tribhuvan University describing her 'exceptionally high level of commitment, motivation, leadership and intellectual abilities' and tireless advocacy for marginalized people and women, and from an ambassador of Nepal describing her as a prominent women activist involved in social work for more than 15 years; and (4) a claim that she helped author the Notice establishing the NWC.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that without documentary evidence to support the claim the petitioner was involved in discussion about CEDAW at the convention or advocated for its adoption within Nepal, counsel's assertions would not satisfy the burden of proof, citing Matter of Obaigbena, Matter of Laureano, and Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez. Participation in conferences as either a trainer or a student does not constitute a contribution without showing how such participation impacted the field as a whole. The letters of recommendation, while providing relevant information, cannot by themselves establish eligibility because they do not demonstrate the alien's work is of major significance beyond individuals she worked with directly. Letters solicited in support of an immigration petition carry less weight than preexisting, independent evidence. Although the letters spoke highly of her contributions, they did not state she made an original contribution of major significance such that the field of social work has changed as a result of her actions. Regarding her claim to have helped author the NWC Notice, she submitted no evidence showing how she participated or what impact her actions had, citing Matter of Soffici and Matter of Treasure Craft of California.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner provided evidence of: (1) an article for and service on the Editorial Board of the Voice for Justice newsletter; (2) 'Women Commission Usefulness and Effectiveness' in Nari Magazine; (3) 'Is Democracy in Danger?' in The Weekly Mirror; (4) 'It's certain to become Woman Prime Minister in Future' in Kumari Magazine; (5) a Chronicle of Events for the Women's National Festival; (6) a booklet about the Trust's activities; (7) various pamphlets not attributed to her; (8) papers entitled 'The Role of Civil Society for Good Governance and Peace' and 'People in Good Governance Planning in Regional Basis: Strategic, Mechanism and Implementation'; (9) a draft paper 'A Celebration of Global Imagination: Listen to the Future'; and (10) a claim of assistance in writing the Notice establishing the NWC.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner submitted no evidence about any of these publications to show they are professional or major trade publications or other major media. The petitioner did not show how the articles relate to her field of social work, as they focus on politics and reports of events. The pamphlets were not attributed to her and there was no evidence they appeared in professional or major trade publications or other major media. The governance papers were not accompanied by certified translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), making them impossible to evaluate. The 'Celebration of Global Imagination' paper was only a draft with no evidence of publication. The AAO cited Matter of Katigbak for the principle that eligibility must be established at the time of filing and cannot be approved based on future facts. Regarding the NWC Notice, it was not attributed to the petitioner, and there was no evidence it was published in any professional publication or other major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed eligibility through her participation with: (1) the NWC; (2) the Nepali Congress Party; (3) the Shanker Memorial Trust; (4) position as Environmental Advisor to the Mayor of Nepalgunj Municipality; (5) coordinator of the Midwest region of the Nepal Women Association; (6) lecturer with Mahendra Multiple Campus of Tribhuvan University; and (7) vice president of the Cricket Association of Nepal (CAN). She submitted a February 8, 2004 letter from an NWC official stating the petitioner 'was by far the most competent' Council member who helped recover a kidnapped woman and assisted with a Canadian government consulting initiative. Another letter described her role in helping to set up the Trust.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner presented no evidence that her work in politics or in the English department at the University relates to her field of social work. Counsel's argument that her involvement with CAN fits within social work because of her nomination as a woman and because sports improve lives was a tenuous connection — almost any organization could be considered social work under this definition, and no objective evidence showed her nomination increased women's visibility. Even assuming the political organizations relate to her field, she did not present evidence these organizations enjoy distinguished reputations — no evidence regarding backgrounds, standing, or any other aspect of their reputations. The February 8, 2004 letter could not by itself establish eligibility because it did not demonstrate the author's knowledge of the Council's reputation independent of her work with the petitioner. Solicited letters carry less weight than preexisting, independent evidence. The petitioner also failed to submit evidence she played a leading or critical role — the letter describing her initiatives did not explain how they furthered the purpose of the Council or constituted a leading role as opposed to the author's role as head of the Council. She submitted no evidence regarding the Nepali Congress Party committees or how her role was distinguishable from other committee chairs, and no evidence regarding her role with other organizations.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed a salary of $24,058.73, compared against the United States State Department Country Profile stating that the per capita income for Nepal in 2005-06 was $322. The salary was composed of employment as a lecturer at Tribhuvan University (nearly 1/3), 'consultancy fees,' 'tuition fees,' and 'project assignments.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the plain language of the criterion requires that the petitioner show she receives a high salary in relation to others in her field, so the annual per capita income from the State Department report is irrelevant even if that information had been included in the record. The petitioner included no evidence regarding how much other social workers earn per annum or any other evidence to demonstrate how her annual salary compares to other social workers in Nepal, so as to demonstrate her stated salary is significantly high in relation to others in her field. The AAO also noted that nearly 1/3 of her stated salary is attributed to her lecturer position, with the remainder from consultancy, tuition, and project assignments, making it unclear which part of her salary is actually related to her claimed field of social work.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding the petitioner failed to meet any of the regulatory criteria she claimed. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished herself to such an extent that she achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of her field. The evidence was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at a national or international level. The burden of proof in visa petition proceedings remains entirely with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and the petitioner did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Katigbak, 14 I. & N. Dec. 45, 49 (Comm. 1971)
Matter of Obaigbena, 19 I. & N. Dec. 533, 534 n.2 (BIA 1988)
Matter of Laureano, 19 I. & N. Dec. 1, 3 n.2 (BIA 1983)
Matter of Ramirez-Sanchez, 17 I. & N. Dec. 503, 506 (BIA 1980)
Matter of Soffici, 22 I. & N. Dec. 158, 165 (Comm. 1998)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I. & N. Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues included: (1) translations of articles in Nari, Manchitra, and Kumari Magazines were incomplete (only excerpts or abstracts) and did not include the author's name, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) and § 204.5(h)(3)(iii); (2) papers on civil society and good governance planning were submitted without certified translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), preventing evaluation; (3) the petitioner was not a member of the NWC, NUTA, or Amnesty International's National Executive Committee at the time of filing, raising eligibility-at-filing issues under Matter of Katigbak; and (4) numerous unsupported assertions of counsel were rejected as not constituting evidence. No fraud or misrepresentation findings were made.
